7hEdited
Our CEO Dario Amodei on why a 10-year AI moratorium without federal backup is too blunt an instrument: "A.I. is advancing too head-spinningly fast. I believe that these systems could change the world, fundamentally, within two years; in 10 years, all bets are off. Without a clear plan for a federal response, a moratorium would give us the worst of both worlds —no ability for states to act, and no national policy as a backstop."
Read the full Guest Essay in the New York Times here:[https://lnkd.in/dgb7tV52](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https://lnkd.in/dgb7tV52&urlhash=NGiq&trk=public_post-text)

2w
Dario Amodei said that he spends up to 40% of his time on culture at Anthropic. Not AI models. Not product strategy. Culture. His reasoning is that in the long run, it's the only thing that actually determines which company wins. I've been sitting with this all week. Most of the leaders I work with have very little time for this kind of work. But I think Amodei is pointing at something important about where leadership is heading. As AI takes on more of the execution, the distinctly human work like building trust, having honest conversations, getting clear on what actually matters becomes a top priority. I've spent the last year working alongside a few teams doing this slowly and intentionally. Meeting after meeting, honest conversation after honest conversation, getting clear on what's most important to them and how to work together to guide their organizations forward. It's the work I find most meaningful. And I have some capacity right now. If you're a leader sitting with a team challenge like trust, communication, a team that's technically functional but not really working together, I'd genuinely love to hear what's going on. Not to pitch you anything. Just to talk. You can reach me at jace@inbo.ca, or find me at inbo.ca.

So what does emotional capacity actually mean in leadership? It’s not softness. It’s not therapy language. It’s not crying in meetings. It’s this: The ability to experience pressure without transmitting panic. That’s it. And you can see it clearly inside real companies. A board challenges the forecast. The CEO stays curious instead of defensive. A top performer pushes back. The CRO asks a better question instead of asserting dominance. A product launch misses. The team investigates patterns instead of searching for a scapegoat. Emotional capacity is the gap between stimulus and reaction. And that gap determines everything. → Whether feedback improves performance or erodes trust → Whether conflict sharpens strategy or fractures teams → Whether scale builds cohesion or chaos The companies that outperform don’t just have better strategy. They have leaders who can: → Hear hard truths without collapsing → Make decisions without theatrics → Hold ambiguity without rushing to false certainty → Regulate their own fear so others don’t have to That is the real compounding advantage. In the age of AI, as information accelerates, emotions accelerate too. And companies inevitably reflect the emotional ceiling of the leadership team. You cannot scale past that ceiling. Full stop. So if growth feels chaotic… reactive… or heavier than it should… It is rarely just a market problem. It is usually a capacity problem. This is where my work sits. At the intersection of human systems and commercial performance. When leaders expand their emotional capacity, organizations stabilize. When the emotional ceiling moves, the revenue ceiling tends to move with it. If you are leading through growth or AI-driven change and want to strengthen the human systems behind performance, feel free to reach out.

Leadership is still about human connection. This week’s Tuesday Reading, by Dr. Christian Schmieder, explores an important leadership reality: even as AI becomes more prevalent, the heart of effective leadership remains human relationships. Technology can help us work faster and make better decisions, but it does not replace the essential, consistent human interactions that build trust, collaboration, and belonging. In environments shaped by digital tools and AI support, leaders must be intentional about sustaining meaningful connections with others. That requires: • Creating space to listen with presence • Prioritizing authentic conversation • Asking questions (and actively listening) to deepen understanding • Remaining emotionally available, not emotionally distant The leaders who thrive with AI are not the ones who automate every task. They are the ones who use technology to free up time for what only humans can do well. They focus their energy on trust, empathy, inclusion, and support. As you think about your own leadership practice this week, consider this: what relationship in your work would benefit from more intentional presence and connection? 🔥 I’ll tell you the exact moment leadership breaks… and it’s not the moment you think. 👇 (If you’ve ever felt yourself tightening under pressure, this one’s going to land.) 🧠 I used to think leadership cracked in the big, dramatic moments. The crisis. The tough call. The room where everyone turns to you. But that’s not where it happens. Leadership breaks in the quiet places, the moments your nervous system gets stretched past what it can hold. ⚠️ It breaks the moment you start tightening instead of trusting. ⚠️ It breaks when you avoid the conversation you know you need to have. ⚠️ It breaks when you perform confidence instead of asking the real question. ⚠️ It breaks when you take something personally that was never about you. None of that looks dramatic from the outside. But inside a team? It changes everything. 💬 People stop telling the truth early. 🧩 Decisions get heavier than they need to be. ⏳ Execution slows because everyone is managing you instead of the work. And here’s the part leaders rarely see until it’s too late: Your team isn’t reacting to the situation. They’re reacting to your regulation. 🧘♂️ When you’re steady, the room settles. 🧭 When you wobble, the room compensates. 🤝 When you stay human under pressure, people follow without needing to be convinced. Tools don’t fix that. Strategy doesn’t fix that. Titles definitely don’t fix that. Capacity does. The internal kind. ✨ The kind you build quietly. ✨ The kind no one applauds. ✨ The kind that holds everything together because you did. If you’ve ever felt yourself tightening in those small moments, you’re not broken. You’re just hitting the edge of your current operating system. And edges? They’re invitations. 🔁 Repost to help another leader find their edge 🔔 Follow Zach Mural PhD for the human‑intelligence layer of leadership and AI adoption

Everyone's optimizing. Nobody's being honest. Leaders right now are being asked to do more with less, transform the org, and keep exhausted people engaged. All at once. Most are coping by toeing the line: AI will bring more creativity, more breathing room, more innovation. Employees know that's not true. Research is starting to confirm it. So when you're the leader saying "AI will make your life better" while every lived experience on your team says the opposite, you're not just wrong. You're eroding the one thing that actually holds a team together right now. Trust. The leaders who win this moment aren't the ones with the best strategy deck. They're the ones willing to say: this is genuinely hard, I see it, and I'm not going to pretend otherwise. That honesty doesn't make you weak. It makes you the signal in a room full of noise.

It feels meaningful to finally share something that began as a quiet question twenty-one years ago. In 2005, I began an inquiry that has evolved throughout my work with leaders: How do we recognize when our work, our decisions, and our lives are truly aligned with who we are? Over the years, that question showed up in conversations, coaching, leadership programs, and moments of reflection with extraordinary people navigating complex decisions. Slowly, something began to take shape: An understanding that alignment itself requires a form of intelligence we rarely name. More recently, that insight resurfaced as I began thinking about AI … Not Artificial Intelligence. Alignment Intelligence The capacity to sense when something is true for you, and when it is not. The abiliry to notice the subtle moment when your words begin to drift away from what you actually believe. The awareness that arises when a decision may look successful on the outside yet feels quietly misaligned on the inside. In my work with leaders, I see something interesting. The most effective leaders are not simply those with the most information. They are those with the highest Alignment Intelligence. They can sense when a strategy is technically correct but humanly disconnected. They can feel when a conversation is avoiding the truth that would actually move things forward. They can recognize when success is beginning to drift away from authenticity. This form of intelligence cannot be automated. It emerges through reflection, attention, and the willingness to listen to the deeper signals that surface when we slow down long enough to notice them. I am so excited to share that I have finally brought these years of inquiry and experience into form as a reflection tool called the Authentic Alignment Compass, a short self-assessment designed to help leaders pause and explore where alignment is present in their work and lives, and where something may be asking for attention. After holding this question for more than two decades, it feels meaningful to finally bring it into form. I feel grateful to begin sharing it with you. You can explore it here: https://lnkd.in/e6vEcXae

Most strategies don’t fail because the plan was wrong. They fail because the organization still believes the old story. A company announces it wants to become more innovative. But managers still believe experimentation is risky. A leadership team commits to adopting AI. But employees still believe AI threatens their jobs. An organization says it wants faster decision-making. But leaders still believe every choice requires consensus. The strategy may be new. But the belief system is old. And belief systems are powerful. They shape how people interpret risk. They determine which ideas get traction. They influence what managers reward, question, or ignore. That’s why transformation is rarely just about new initiatives. It’s about changing the narrative inside the organization. The most effective leaders understand this. They reinforce new ideas consistently. They bring in outside perspectives that challenge assumptions. They repeat the message until it becomes the new normal. Because strategy doesn’t truly succeed when it’s announced. It succeeds when people start thinking differently. Curious how others approach this. When your organization is trying to change direction, what actually shifts the internal mindset?#Leadership#StrategyExecution#OrganizationalChange#ExecutiveLeadership#FutureOfWork

In Chapter 3 of my book I lay out the AIR framework, a practical leadership playbook for the AI era, and it is the heart of building an innovation culture. Three short ideas that change the game: - Agility: leaders must shrink feedback loops; reward fast experiments over polished plans. Create structures where teams can sense, try, and learn every sprint. - Intelligence: treat data and AI as partners, not magic substitutes. Leaders translate insight into decision clarity, they define when machines suggest and when humans decide. - Resilience: normalize recovery and learning, not perfection. Resilient teams recover faster, adapt policy faster, and sustain creativity under pressure. Trust underpins all three. Without psychological safety and clear accountability, AI amplifies fear, not creativity. My Trust Playbook shows how to codify boundaries between automation and human judgment, so people can innovate without losing control. Practical moves any leader can start this week: - Map decision rights, then remove the 1 or 2 blockers that slow every experiment. - Run a 2-week micro-experiment, measure learning not vanity metrics, share the playbook companywide. - Publish a failure journal, celebrate what was learned, and lock in one process improvement. - Use tools that make learning visible; the right app can track experiments, map decision owners, surface trust metrics, and run lightweight scenario simulations with AI. These features turn good intentions into repeatable routines. Leadership is not a title; it is the daily practice of shaping context. Strategy is not a static plan; it is the set of choices that create permission to experiment. Combine both and innovation becomes less accidental and more reliable. Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei on Being an Underdog, AI Safety and Economic Inequality

Photograph by Ian Allen for TIME
By Billy Perrigo
Want more from TIME in your inbox? Explore our other newsletters here.
Hanging on the wall of Anthropic’s offices in San Francisco in early May, a stone’s throw from the conference room where CEO Dario Amodei would shortly sit for an interview with TIME, was a framed meme. Its single panel showed a giant robot ransacking a burning city. Underneath, the image’s tongue-in-cheek title: Deep learning is hitting a wall . That’s a refrain you often hear from AI skeptics, who claim that rapid progress in artificial intelligence will soon taper off. But in the image, an arrow points to the robot, labeling it “deep learning.” Another points to the devastated city: “wall.”
The cheeky meme sums up the attitude inside Anthropic, an AI company where most employees seem to believe that AI progress isn’t slowing down—and could potentially result in dangerous outcomes for humanity. Amodei, who was on the cover of TIME last month as his company was named one of TIME’s 100 Most Influential Companies of 2024, says Anthropic is devoted to studying cutting-edge AI systems and developing new safety methods. And yet Anthropic is also a leading competitor to OpenAI, releasing powerful tools for use by the public and businesses, in what even Amodei himself has worried might be a perilous race that could end badly.
On June 20, Anthropic fired its latest broadside in that race, releasing the latest version of its Claude chatbot: Claude 3.5 Sonnet. The model, according to the company, sets new industry standards on reasoning, coding and some types of math, beating GPT-4o, the most recent offering from OpenAI. “With today's launch, we're taking a step towards what we believe could be a significant shift in how we interact with technology,” Amodei said in a statement on June 20. “Our goal with Claude isn't to create an incrementally better [large language model] but to develop an AI system that can work alongside people and software in meaningful ways.”
TIME’s cover story about Amodei last month delved into the question of whether Anthropic can succeed at its safety mission while under pressure to compete with OpenAI, as well as tech giants like Microsoft, Google and Amazon—the latter two of whom are significant investors in Anthropic. Today, TIME is publishing a longer excerpt from the interview conducted in early May for that piece. It has been condensed and edited for clarity.

Certainly all the facts you cite are true. But it’s becoming less true. We have these big compute deals. We have high single-digit billions in funding. Whereas our competitors have low double-digits to, somewhat frighteningly, triple-digits. So I’m starting to feel a little bit awkward about calling ourselves the underdogs. But relative to the heavyweights in the field, I think it’s absolutely true.

We released our last model relatively recently, so it’s not like there’s a year of unreleased secrets. And to the extent that there are, I’m not going to go into them. But we do know enough to say that the progress is continuing. We don’t see any evidence that things are leveling off. The reality of the world we live in is that it could stop any time. Every time we train a new model, I look at it and I’m always wondering—I’m never sure in relief or concern—[if] at some point we’ll see, oh man, the model doesn’t get any better. I think if [the effects of scaling] did stop, in some ways that would be good for the world. It would restrain everyone at the same time. But it’s not something we get to choose—and of course the models bring many exciting benefits. Mostly, it’s a fact of nature. We don’t get to choose, we just get to find out which world we live in, and then deal with it as best we can.

We have a donation matching program. [Anthropic allows employees to donate up to 25% of their equity to any charity, and will match the donation.] That’s inclined to attract employees for whom public benefit is appealing. Our stock grants look like a better deal if that’s something you value. It doesn’t prevent people from having financial incentives, but it helps to set the tone.
In terms of the safety side of things, there’s a little bit of a delta between the public perception and what we have in mind. I think of us less as an AI safety company, and more think of us as a company that’s focused on public benefit. We’re not a company that believes a certain set of things about the dangers that AI systems are going to have. That’s an empirical question. I more want Anthropic to be a company where everyone is thinking about the public purpose, rather than a one-issue company that’s focused on AI safety or the misalignment of AI systems. Internally I think we’ve succeeded at that, where we have people with a bunch of different perspectives, but what they share is a real commitment to the public purpose.

Look, whoever the next president is, we’re going to work with them to do the best we can to explain both that the U.S. needs to stay ahead of its adversaries in this technology, but also that we need to provide reasonable safeguards on the technology itself. My message is going to be the same. Obviously different administrations are going to have different views, and I expect different [government] policies depending on the outcome of the election. All I can really do is say what I think is true about the world.

I don’t think it’s inherently flimsy—I would more say that we’re early in the science of how to steer these systems. Early in that they hallucinate, early in that they can be jailbroken. Early in the sense that, every time we want to make our model more friendly sounding, then it’ll turn out that in some other context, it gives responses that are too long. You’re trying to make it do all the good things, and not all the bad things, but that’s just hard to do. I think it’s not perfect, and I think it’ll probably never be perfect. We have a Swiss cheese model: you can have one layer that has holes in it, but if you have 10 layers of Swiss cheese, it’s hard for anything to fly through all 10 layers. There’s no silver bullet that’s going to help us steer the models. We’re going to have to put a lot of different things together.

Dissecting AI Superalignment: OpenAI, Anthropic and… Vibranium Holdings 1 year ago
Coffee with AI - July 21, 2025 - Edition #215 Alexandre Messina 8 months ago
DeepSeek & OpenAI – Plato’s Cave and the Mystery of AI… Chris McLeod 1 year ago

I’d prefer to live in an ideal world. Unfortunately in the world we actually live in, there’s a lot of economic pressure, there’s not only competition between companies, there’s competition between nations. But if we can really demonstrate that the risks are real, which I hope to be able to do, then there may be moments when we can really get the world to stop and consider for at least a little bit. And do this right in a cooperative way. But I’m not naive—those moments are rare, and halting a very powerful economic train is something that can only be done for a short period of time in extraordinary circumstances.
Read more: Inside Anthropic, the AI Company Betting That Safety Can Be a Winning Strategy

Our concern is not primarily about open-source models, it’s about powerful models. In practice, what I think will happen with most of these companies—I suspect it will happen with Meta, but I’m not certain—is that they’ll stop open-sourcing below the level where I have concerns. I ultimately suspect that open-source as an issue is almost a red herring. People treat open-source models as if they undermine our business model. The truth is, our competitors are anyone who can produce a powerful model. When someone hosts one of these models—and I think we should call them open weights models—they get put on the cloud and the economics are the same as everything else.

I think a thing that has been prominent in our culture has been “do more with less.” We always try to maintain a situation where, with less computational resources, we can do the same or better than someone who has many more computational resources. So in the end, our competitive advantage is our creativity as researchers, as engineers. I think increasingly in terms of creative product offerings, rather than pure compute. I think you need a lot of pure compute. We have a lot, and we're gonna get more. But as long as we can use it more efficiently, as long as we can do more with less, then in the end, the resources are going to find their way to the innovative companies.

I think we’re pretty good for the one after the next one as well, although in the history of Anthropic, we’re going to need to raise more. It’s hard to say where that [will come from]. There are traditional investors. There are large compute providers. And there are miscellaneous other sources that we may not have thought of.

The technology is getting powerful enough that government should have an important role in its creation and deployment. This is not just about regulation. This is about national security. At some point, should at least some aspects of this be a national project? I certainly think there are going to be lots of uses for models within government. But I do think that as time goes on, it’s possible that the most powerful models will be thought of as national-security-critical. I think governments can become much more involved, and I’m interested in having democratic governments lead this technology in a responsible and accountable way. And that could mean that governments are heavily involved in the building of this technology. I don’t know what that would look like. Nothing like that exists today. But given how powerful the thing we’re building is, should individuals or even companies really be at the center of it once it gets to a certain strength? I could imagine in a small number of years—two, three, five years—that could be a very serious conversation, depending on what the situation is.

I would say so. We have billions of people who still live on less than $1 per day. That just doesn’t seem good.

There’s two separate things there. There’s lifting the floor, and then there's power within the world. I think I'm a little less sanguine about this concentration of power. Even if we materially provide for everyone, it's hard for democracies and democratic decision-making to exist and to be fair, if power is concentrated too much. Financial concentration of power is one form of power, and there are others. But there were periods in U.S. history—look at the Gilded Age—where industrialists essentially took over government. I think that's probably too extreme. I'm not sure the economy can function without some amount of inequality. But I'm concerned we might be in one of those eras, where it's trending towards being too extreme to be healthy for a democratic polity.
Ideas around guaranteed basic income—if we can't think of anything better, I certainly think that's better than nothing. But I would much prefer a world in which everyone can contribute. It would be kind of dystopian if there are these few people that can make trillions of dollars, and then the government hands it all out to the unwashed masses. It’s better than not handing it out, but I think it's not really the world we want to aim for. I do believe that if the exponential [rate of AI progress] is right, AI systems will be better than most humans, maybe all humans, at doing most of the things humans do. And so we're really going to need to rethink a lot. In the short run, I always talk about complementarity—we should make sure humans and AIs work together. That's a great answer in the short run. I think in the long run, we're really going to need to think about, how do we organize the economy, and how humans think about their lives? One person can't do that. One company can't do that. That’s a conversation among humanity. And my only worry is, if the technology goes fast, we’ll have to figure it out fast.

Strong point by Amodei. This is exactly why company culture matters so much in AI. Not only because culture shapes collaboration, trust, and long-term thinking, but also because it determines whether different kinds of minds can actually perform at their highest level. And this is where neurodiversity becomes a serious topic, not a side topic. A lot of people still reduce ADHD or autism to shallow comments like “everyone has ADHD now” or “people just self-diagnose.” In many cases, that sounds more like lack of understanding than real knowledge. AI and adjacent technical fields often seem to be a better fit for neurodivergent minds than many traditional environments, and that is still not discussed enough. Different cognitive styles can be a real asset in complex problem-solving, systems thinking, and innovation — but only if the culture is mature enough to recognize and support them instead of misreading them. So yes — culture may be one of the biggest competitive advantages. Because the companies that truly understand human cognitive diversity will likely be better at unlocking exceptional talent.

I recently read “The Adolescence of Technology” by Dario Amodei, CEO and co-founder of Anthropic. In my view, it is one of the most comprehensive and clear-headed analyses to date of the risks associated with increasingly powerful AI systems. What I found particularly valuable is the framing: advanced AI as a technology in an “adolescent” phase — already highly capable, but not yet matched by equally mature governance, safeguards, or societal understanding. The essay is long, but for anyone looking beyond hype or simplistic narratives, it is well worth the time. Amodei outlines five concrete categories of risk: - Misuse for large-scale harm: AI could lower barriers to creating or deploying destructive capabilities, amplifying the impact of malicious actors. - Misuse for concentration of power: Powerful AI may enable unprecedented surveillance, manipulation, and centralized control. - Autonomy and alignment risks: As systems grow more capable, ensuring reliable alignment with human intent becomes increasingly challenging. - Economic disruption: Rapid productivity gains may coincide with significant labor displacement and social strain. - Systemic and unknown risks: Beyond identifiable threats, advanced AI may introduce emergent and hard-to-predict second-order effects.#AI#ArtificialIntelligence#Leadership#AIGovernance#TechnologyRisk#Anthropic#Management#FutureOfWork https://lnkd.in/eAJZG5HW

In the mid 1990s as the internet was exponentially becoming a utility for humanity, experts were deeply divided on whether it would become a force for good or a societal risk. Some, like Jim Metcalfe, Chairman of Netscape said in 1995: “The Internet isn’t what I think of as a fad. It’s 40 million people plus right now, growing at 8% a month. You know if it’s a fad, also bear in mind that data communications is going to be the essence of what we do in the future so why would something that’s giving you low cost data communications be a fad. I think it’s here to stay just as the phone system was”. Others worried about privacy and surveillance citing a risk of the internet tracking users, selling databases of consumer behavior, and invading privacy for profit. Today this debate rages about whether AI can deliver the transformational potential promised by its founders or if it will morph into an irresistible force that leads to a dystopian future for humanity. #AI, Digital Platforms, and the New Systemic Risk by Philipp Hacker (European University Viadrina Frankfurt) and Atoosa Kasirzadeh (Carnegie Mellon University) explores the emerging landscape of systemic risk in AI and platform governance. The paper develops a comprehensive framework to understand how interconnected AI systems, digital platforms, and hybrid structures pose complex, large-scale risks. It critically evaluates current legislation, including the EU's AI Act and Digital Services Act (DSA), highlighting gaps in definitions and risk assessments. The authors identify novel risk pathways such as systemic failures from AI interactions and propose reforms to broaden risk evaluation, improve regulatory coordination, and address collective harms. This work provides vital insights for policymakers, regulators, and stakeholders aiming to navigate the evolving challenges of AI governance in interconnected societies. 8 things I took away from the Dario Dwarkesh interview, which gave some useful snapshots into the applied worldview of one of the most important leaders in AI: 1/ Dario is still confident that his “country of geniuses in a data center” from Machines of Loving Grace will happen: hasn't yet, but he thinks “safe bet” (~90% likely) within 10 years, and 50/50 within 1-3 years. And that time delay for economic diffusion is real, but “will still be really fast.” 2/ While Dario is very bullish as always on the power of these “geniuses,” he admitted uncertainty on how well they'll perform on tasks that are harder to verify (than, say, math and coding) or lack an easy objective function. I think this is especially relevant for professional domains such as finance and management. 3/ Dario emphasized the distinction between AI writing 90 or 100% of code, vs. AI doing 90 or 100% of all software engineering tasks. (He thinks all of these will happen in quick succession, but stresses that these are four different thresholds.) Worth keeping in mind as people (including me) go gaga over the SemiAnalysis Claude Code chart. 4/ Dwarkesh asked: why train on skills like XLS and Slack if general super-intelligence is coming? Dario said, at least from @Anthropic’s point of view, the primary reason is NOT for those specific skills, but rather because they believe learning on a greater breadth of skills will help the models generalize more broadly (as played out with text in the early days of LLMs). 5/ Dwarkesh asked: don't we need continual learning to get to the “country of geniuses” and trillions of dollars of AI revenue? Dario thinks maybe we get there with scale plus in-context learning alone. Anthropic is working on continual learning too, and Dario stated there is a “good chance” they solve that in 1-2 years as well. E.g., via longer context, which Dario sees as an engineering problem more than a fundamental research problem. 6/ "[At Anthropic] the pressure to survive economically while also keeping our values is just incredible... there is zero time for bullshit, there is zero time for feeling like we are productive when we are not." (Answering whether the coding productivity uplift from AI is real… with conviction that Anthropic has a 15-20% advantage on this today, and that this will keep growing.) 7/ Dwarkesh asked: if so bullish on the tech, why not invest even more in data centers? Dario answered that given the uncertainty around pace of economic diffusion (e.g., does it take 1, 2, 5 years to get to trillions of $ of revenue?) and the magnitude of the investment, being just a bit off could lead to bankruptcy… so they need to be conservative. 8/ For the Anthropic investors and wannabe investors out there: “We are an enterprise business... less fickle than consumers, and better margins.” I’d add that beyond product quality, I expect trust to be a driver of enterprise customer retention and price premium. https://lnkd.in/ehc7ah8g

If you want to develop a better understanding of the AI space, do spend 150 minutes Used Gemini to create a summary * The End of the Exponential: Amodei argues that we are approaching the final stages of the current exponential growth curve in AI capabilities. He expresses surprise that there isn't more public recognition of how close we are to achieving "Country of Geniuses" level intelligence in data centers. * The Scaling Hypothesis & RL: The "Big Blob of Compute" hypothesis remains central. Amodei explains that the same scaling laws that applied to pre-training are now being observed in Reinforcement Learning (RL), where model performance on verifiable tasks (like math and code) improves log-linearly with training time. * Verifiable vs. Non-Verifiable Tasks: AI progress is fastest in domains where results can be objectively verified, such as coding and mathematics. Amodei notes that while generalization to non-verifiable tasks (like writing a novel or strategic planning) is happening, there is more fundamental uncertainty in those areas. * AI Training vs. Human Evolution: Amodei suggests that AI pre-training and RL exist in a "middle space" between human evolution (the development of priors) and short-term human learning. While humans are more sample-efficient, AI models make up for it with massive context windows and vast data ingestion. * Economic Diffusion Lag: A major theme is the gap between AI capability and economic diffusion. Amodei argues that even if a "Country of Geniuses" exists in a lab by 2026 or 2027, it will take years to manifest in the broader economy due to legal, regulatory, and change-management hurdles. * The Automation of Software Engineering: Amodei outlines a spectrum of automation for developers, predicting that AI will move from writing 90% of code to handling 90% of end-to-end software engineering tasks (including environment setup and technical direction) within a few years. * AI Safety and "Adolescence of Technology": The interview touches on the risks outlined in Amodei's writings, specifically regarding biological weapons, autonomy risk, and the need for "transparency standards" to monitor models as they become capable of causing large-scale harm. * Geopolitics and the "Democracy Advantage": Amodei advocates for a coalition of democratic nations to hold the "strongest hand" in AI development. He expresses concern that AI could empower high-tech authoritarianism and suggests that export controls on chips are necessary to maintain a favorable "initial condition" for the global order. * The Economics of AI Firms: Amodei discusses the "hellish demand prediction problem" facing AI labs. He explains how the massive capital expenditure required for data centers creates a volatile financial environment where being off by just one year in growth projections can lead to bankruptcy. https://lnkd.in/gzBEgs_E

AI and Global Order: The interview delves into the geopolitical stakes of AI. The Scaling Hypothesis Dario’s core belief hasn’t changed since 2017 — the “Big Blob of Compute Hypothesis.” Progress comes from raw compute, data quality, training duration, and scalable objective functions. He sees RL scaling following the same log-linear pattern as pre-training. Timeline to AGI He’s 90% confident we get a “country of geniuses in a data center” within 10 years, with a 50/50 hunch it’s more like 1-3 years. He predicts trillions in revenue before 2030, and end-to-end software engineering fully automated within 1-2 years. Economic Diffusion vs Capability AI progress is fast but not infinitely fast. Adoption lags capability due to procurement cycles, compliance, and change management. Anthropic’s own 10x annual growth (~$100M → $1B → $9-10B) is evidence of fast but bounded diffusion. Continual Learning He’s skeptical this is a real blocker. Pre-training and RL generalization may simply get there without it — the codebase itself serves as external memory. Compute Investment Strategy Anthropic doesn’t buy maximum compute because being off by even one year in demand forecasting is financially ruinous. The industry is collectively scaling to multiple trillions annually by 2028-2029. Geopolitics and AI He’s concerned about authoritarian governments gaining AGI-level capability. Initial conditions when “rules of the road” are set will matter enormously — he wants democratic nations holding the stronger hand at that moment. Regulation He opposed the 10-year federal moratorium on state AI laws but supports federal preemption with actual standards. Near-term focus should be transparency; targeted safety legislation should follow as risks crystallize. He’s less worried about chatbot bills, more worried about the FDA pipeline getting jammed by AI-accelerated drug discovery. Business Model The API model is durable because there’s always a frontier of new use cases. “Pay for results” pricing will emerge alongside it. Claude Code succeeded because Anthropic itself was the first heavy user. Company Culture He spends 30-40% of his time on culture — biweekly all-hands (“Dario Vision Quests”), honest Slack communication, no corporate defensiveness. He sees this as a major competitive advantage over rivals experiencing internal decoherence. https://lnkd.in/daC39YX6

These questions keep coming up in recent AI conversations: 🔹 Can frontier LLMs be scaled to reach AGI?, and more consequentially 🔹 Do LLMs need to emulate human intelligence to get there? In a recent conversation with Dario Amodei (CEO@Anthropic) Dwarkesh challenges him by claiming that LLMs lack the core human learning algorithm because they need trillions of tokens to learn useful capabilities. Dario argues that the comparison is misleading: LLM training compensates for billions of years of evolution plus the human embodied experience. The argument I would like to use is that LLMs may represent a genuinely different kind of intelligence. Human cognition is one particular design, not a necessary blueprint for intelligence in general. So instead of asking “Why don’t LLMs learn like us?”, we might ask: 🔹 In what ways LLMs are a different kind of intelligence? 🔹 To what extent different intelligence is fine? Which raises the question: what is intelligence anyway? OpenAI defines intelligence through the lens of Artificial General Intelligence (AGI), which it describes as highly autonomous systems that outperform humans at most economically valuable work. Francois Chollet proposed a very different perspective: intelligence as skill acquisition efficiency. How efficiently a system converts experience + prior knowledge into new skills, especially in novel situations. That's a much stronger requirement. It shifts the focus from "how many things you can do" to "how well you can learn new things". Humans are actually very good at this kind of generalization to novelty. Proven by the wide range of skills any human learns in our modern world with striking sample efficiently. But should we care to reach intelligence by Chollet if LLMs can outperform humans at most economically valuable work? And is it even possible to get there without mastering Chollet's version? P.S: I want to revisit this through the lens of coding assistants and other knowledge-work domains. https://lnkd.in/dtNujetY

[Global Insights] Dario Amodei: Machine of love

Dario Amodei is the CEO of Anthropic, a public benefit corporation dedicated to building AI systems that are steerable, interpretable and safe.
Previously, Dario served as Vice President of Research at OpenAI, where he led the development of large language models like GPT-2 and GPT-3. He is also the co-inventor of reinforcement learning from human feedback. Before joining OpenAI, he worked at Google Brain as a Senior Research Scientist.

This summary outlines Dario Amodei’s interview on the potential benefits of AI, compiling his core insights, along with the original text for reference.
- The positive potential of AI is severely underestimated.
Amodei believes that most people underestimate the positive aspects of powerful AI, just as they underestimate its risks. He is committed to studying AI risks because it helps us avoid them and ultimately achieve a positive future.
★ "I often think and talk about the risks of powerful AI. The company I serve as CEO, Anthropic, has conducted extensive research on how to reduce these risks. Because of this, people sometimes conclude that I’m a pessimist or a 'doomsayer,' thinking that AI is mostly bad or dangerous. I don’t believe that at all. In fact, one of the main reasons I focus on risks is that they are the only obstacles between us and the fundamentally positive future I see. I think most people severely underestimate how radical the positive aspects of AI can be, just as I believe most people underestimate the seriousness of the risks."
- Focusing on AI risks is about maximizing leverage for positive factors, not pessimism.
Amodei explains why Anthropic focuses more on risks rather than the positive aspects of AI. The core reason lies in the control of risks and avoiding negative perceptions. AI’s positive development seems inevitable, while risks can be reduced through effort.
★ "I made this choice particularly for the following reasons: maximizing leverage. The fundamental development of AI technology and many (not all) of its benefits seem inevitable (unless risks ruin everything), and are fundamentally driven by powerful market forces. On the other hand, risks are not predetermined, and our actions can greatly change their likelihood. Avoiding the perception of promotion. AI companies talking about all the amazing benefits of AI can come across as promotional or as if they’re trying to distract from the downsides. I also think, in principle, spending too much time 'defending your position' is bad for your soul."
- Powerful AI will surpass human experts and autonomously complete complex tasks.
Amodei defines “powerful AI,” whose capabilities will far exceed top human experts and autonomously complete tasks that take days or even weeks.
★ "The powerful AI I envision is a model—possibly similar in form to today’s LLMs, though it might be based on a different architecture, involve multiple interacting models, and be trained in different ways—that possesses the following characteristics: in terms of sheer intellect, it is smarter than Nobel laureates in most relevant fields (biology, programming, mathematics, engineering, writing, etc.), which means it can prove unsolved mathematical theorems, write great novels, code complex software from scratch, and more. Beyond being a 'smart entity to talk to,' it has access to all 'interfaces' available to humans, such as text, audio, video, mouse, and keyboard control, as well as internet access."
- AI's impact on technological progress is neither instant nor trivial, but a complex process.
Amodei believes that AI’s impact on the world won’t happen overnight, nor is it trivial; it’s limited by factors such as the speed of the physical world, data acquisition, inherent complexity, human limitations, and the laws of physics.
- AI will bring revolutionary breakthroughs in biology and medicine, achieving a “compressed 21st century.”
Amodei predicts that AI will significantly accelerate progress in biology and medicine, compressing the advances of the next 50-100 years into 5-10 years, resulting in a “compressed 21st century.” This will manifest in disease prevention, treatment, and human lifespan extension.
- AI will profoundly change the field of neuroscience and mental health.
Amodei believes AI will accelerate neuroscience progress through traditional molecular biology, precise neural measurement and intervention, advanced computational neuroscience, and behavioral intervention, potentially curing most mental illnesses and enhancing human cognitive and emotional capabilities.
- AI has the potential to address issues of economic development and poverty, but many challenges must be overcome.
Amodei is cautiously optimistic about whether AI can solve inequality and economic growth problems in developing countries. He believes AI can optimize resource allocation and accelerate economic growth but must overcome challenges like corruption and weak institutions.
- AI can play a role in international peace and governance, but the victory of democracy is not guaranteed.
Amodei believes AI can improve governance efficiency, but the victory of democracy is not guaranteed. Active efforts are needed to maintain and promote democratic systems. He proposes a “coordination strategy,” where democratic alliances maintain global democracy through technological advantages and diplomacy.
- The AI era requires a rethinking of work and meaning.
Amodei points out that the AI era requires society to rethink work and meaning. Although AI may replace many jobs, humans can still find new sources of value and meaning, requiring extensive social discussion and adjustment.

【I think and talk a lot about the risks of powerful AI. The company I’m the CEO of, Anthropic, does a lot of research on how to reduce these risks. Because of this, people sometimes draw the conclusion that I’m a pessimist or “doomer” who thinks AI will be mostly bad or dangerous. I don’t think that at all. In fact, one of my main reasons for focusing on risks is that they’re the only thing standing between us and what I see as a fundamentally positive future. I think that most people are underestimating just how radical the upside of AI could be, just as I think most people are underestimating how bad the risks could be.
In this essay I try to sketch out what that upside might look like—what a world with powerful AI might look like if everything goes right.
First, however, I wanted to briefly explain why I and Anthropic haven’t talked that much about powerful AI’s upsides, and why we’ll probably continue, overall, to talk a lot about risks. In particular, I’ve made this choice out of a desire to:
-Maximize leverage. The basic development of AI technology and many (not all) of its benefits seems inevitable (unless the risks derail everything) and is fundamentally driven by powerful market forces. On the other hand, the risks are not predetermined and our actions can greatly change their likelihood.
-Avoid perception of propaganda. AI companies talking about all the amazing benefits of AI can come off like propagandists, or as if they’re attempting to distract from downsides. I also think that as a matter of principle it’s bad for your soul to spend too much of your time “talking your book”.
-Avoid grandiosity. I am often turned off by the way many AI risk public figures (not to mention AI company leaders) talk about the post-AGI world, as if it’s their mission to single-handedly bring it about like a prophet leading their people to salvation. I think it’s dangerous to view companies as unilaterally shaping the world, and dangerous to view practical technological goals in essentially religious terms.
-Avoid “sci-fi” baggage. Although I think most people underestimate the upside of powerful AI, the small community of people who do discuss radical AI futures often does so in an excessively “sci-fi” tone (featuring e.g. uploaded minds, space exploration, or general cyberpunk vibes). I think this causes people to take the claims less seriously, and to imbue them with a sort of unreality. To be clear, the issue isn’t whether the technologies described are possible or likely (the main essay discusses this in granular detail)—it’s more that the “vibe” connotatively smuggles in a bunch of cultural baggage and unstated assumptions about what kind of future is desirable, how various societal issues will play out, etc. The result often ends up reading like a fantasy for a narrow subculture, while being off-putting to most people.
Yet despite all of the concerns above, I really do think it’s important to discuss what a good world with powerful AI could look like, while doing our best to avoid the above pitfalls. In fact I think it is critical to have a genuinely inspiring vision of the future, and not just a plan to fight fires. Many of the implications of powerful AI are adversarial or dangerous, but at the end of it all, there has to be something we’re fighting for, some positive-sum outcome where everyone is better off, something to rally people to rise above their squabbles and confront the challenges ahead. Fear is one kind of motivator, but it’s not enough: we need hope as well.
The list of positive applications of powerful AI is extremely long (and includes robotics, manufacturing, energy, and much more), but I’m going to focus on a small number of areas that seem to me to have the greatest potential to directly improve the quality of human life. The five categories I am most excited about are:
1. Biology and physical health
2. Neuroscience and mental health
3. Economic development and poverty
4. Peace and governance
5. Work and meaning
My predictions are going to be radical as judged by most standards (other than sci-fi “singularity” visions2), but I mean them earnestly and sincerely. Everything I’m saying could very easily be wrong (to repeat my point from above), but I’ve at least attempted to ground my views in a semi-analytical assessment of how much progress in various fields might speed up and what that might mean in practice. I am fortunate to have professional experience in both biology and neuroscience, and I am an informed amateur in the field of economic development, but I am sure I will get plenty of things wrong. One thing writing this essay has made me realize is that it would be valuable to bring together a group of domain experts (in biology, economics, international relations, and other areas) to write a much better and more informed version of what I’ve produced here. It’s probably best to view my efforts here as a starting prompt for that group.】
To read the full text: Machines of Loving Grace: How AI Could Transform the World for the Better https://darioamodei.com/machines-of-loving-grace

Ever heard of a company declaring a "Code Red"? It sounds like something out of a sci-fi movie, but when OpenAI did it, it was less about immediate disaster and more about hitting the gas on safety! Think of it this way: their AI models, like GPT-4, are getting so incredibly smart, so fast, that the folks at OpenAI realized they needed to seriously level up their safety game to keep pace with the progress. It's a proactive measure—a big, internal alarm to prioritize safety protocols and research before their cutting-edge AI takes an unexpected turn. This urgency is mainly centered on two big concepts: Alignment and Advanced Capabilities. Alignment is essentially making sure that the AI's goals match human goals—we want our digital buddies to be helpful, not accidentally turn into super-villains while trying to optimize global paperclip production! Meanwhile, Advanced Capabilities means as the AI can do more complex things, the potential for unintended side effects or even misuse grows. So, the "code red" is their way of saying, "Hold up, let's make doubly sure these brilliant machines are going to be good citizens." So, what does this drama mean for you and me? Surprisingly, it's actually great news! This heightened focus means the AI tools we use will likely become safer, more ethical, and more reliable. Instead of worrying, we should see this as a sign that the leading innovators are being incredibly responsible. They're not just rushing to build the future; they're making sure the foundation is rock-solid. It means we're heading toward a future where powerful AI is built with human safety right at the core—and that's a future I can definitely get behind!

Dario Amodei, CEO of Anthropic, addresses the implications of DeepSeek’s recent advancements in AI.
He outlines three key dynamics in AI development:
1.	Scaling Laws: Increasing the scale of AI training leads to progressively better results across various cognitive tasks.
2.	Shifting the Curve: Continuous innovations in model architecture and hardware efficiency shift the performance curve, enabling better results at reduced costs.
3.	Shifting the Paradigm: Occasionally, new training methodologies, such as reinforcement learning for generating chains of thought, emerge, enhancing performance on specific tasks.

Co-Founder of Anthropic released a statement on Deep Seek's compute cost savings claim, for the business people in turmoil. Excepts:
1. DeepSeek actually had 50,000 Hopper generation chips6, which I'd guess is within a factor \~2-3x of what the major US AI companies have (for example, it's 2-3x less than the xAI "Colossus" cluster)7. Those 50,000 Hopper chips cost on the order of \~$1B. Thus, DeepSeek's total spend as a company (as distinct from spend to train an individual model) is not vastly different from US AI labs.
2. DeepSeek does not "do for $6M5 what cost US AI companies billions". I can only speak for Anthropic, but Claude 3.5 Sonnet is a mid-sized model that cost a few $10M's to train (I won't give an exact number). Also, 3.5 Sonnet was not trained in any way that involved a larger or more expensive model (contrary to some rumors). Sonnet's training was conducted 9-12 months ago, and DeepSeek's model was trained in November/December, while Sonnet remains notably ahead in many internal and external evals. Thus, I think a fair statement is "DeepSeek produced a model close to the performance of US models 7-10 months older, for a good deal less cost (but not anywhere near the ratios people have suggested)".[https://lnkd.in/dFDiSXg4](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https://lnkd.in/dFDiSXg4&urlhash=_xEB&trk=public_post-text)

When I first heard about DeepSeek's efficiency improvements, two key thoughts came to mind:
1️⃣ These improvements seemed to follow the expected trajectory in LLM development, where we've consistently seen dramatic increases in performance/cost ratios
2️⃣ Even if these efficiency gains were revolutionary, they wouldn't reduce the massive chip demands or investment in AI - they'd likely increase them by offering more compelling returns on investment
I found an article by the CEO of Anthropic which articulates these thoughts much better than I can (see link below). His analysis shows that:
•DeepSeek's cost reductions (\~3-4x cheaper than 9-12 month old models) align with the historical \~4x yearly efficiency improvements in AI
•Their V3 model's performance matches where you'd expect compared to US models from 7-10 months ago
•The R1 model's reasoning capabilities, while impressive, reflect the industry's current focus on RL-based chain-of-thought training - similar to OpenAI's o1-preview
Key takeaway: Better efficiency in AI doesn't mean we'll use fewer resources - it means we'll use the same (or more) resources to build even more powerful models. The appetite for compute and investment only grows.
Thoughts?

In his recent post, Dario Amodei highlights how AI breakthroughs, like those seen with DeepSeek, are part of an ongoing, collaborative and iterative process in model development.
He outlines three key dynamics driving AI advancement:
1️⃣ Scaling Laws –More investment in training leads to better AI performance.
2️⃣ Shifting the Curve –Advances in architecture and hardware efficiency make AI development cheaper and more effective.
3️⃣ Shifting the Paradigm –New methodologies, like reinforcement learning, can unlock step-change improvements.
What’s crucial to understand is that AI progress is not linear or one-sided—it’s a democratic process. Chinese and American researchers (and many others worldwide) build on each other’s work, refine models, and push the field forward. Some breakthroughs will happen elsewhere, and that’s expected and normal.
📌The key takeaway? The only way to stay ahead is to keep learning, innovating, and refining. AI isn’t a zero-sum game—it’s an ongoing race of understanding, experimentation, and progress.
🔎Impact for consumers: Expect faster, cheaper, and more powerful AI models driving innovation across industries—from healthcare to finance to automation. The future is about who iterates fastest, not just who gets there first.[#AI](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/ai&trk=public_post-text)[#Innovation](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/innovation&trk=public_post-text)[#GlobalResearch](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/globalresearch&trk=public_post-text)[#DemocratizingAI](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/democratizingai&trk=public_post-text)[#TechEvolution](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/techevolution&trk=public_post-text)[https://lnkd.in/eFSZG4bj](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https://lnkd.in/eFSZG4bj&urlhash=w78g&trk=public_post-text)

I wanted to share[Dario Amodei](https://www.linkedin.com/in/dario-amodei-3934934?trk=public_post-text)'s recent post on DeepSeek’s AI progress. There’s been a lot of talk that DeepSeek might’ve jumped way ahead of U.S. labs at a fraction of the cost, but Dario shows it’s basically in line with existing trends—especially around cost improvements, reinforcement learning, and why export controls still matter.
We agree with much of what[Dario Amodei](https://www.linkedin.com/in/dario-amodei-3934934?trk=public_post-text)and[Anthropic](https://www.linkedin.com/company/anthropicresearch?trk=public_post-text)are saying. We at[Canyon GBS](https://www.linkedin.com/company/canyongbs?trk=public_post-text)also see that real applied AI innovation will hinge on how we actually put great models to work. It’s not only about the data science—combining AI with better software, and novel strategies in applying AI is what makes AI truly useful in everyday problem solving.
Why it matters:
➡️It helps us see past the hype about DeepSeek’s “big leap.”
➡️It reminds us export controls aren’t about halting progress, but ensuring it’s handled responsibly.
➡️It highlights that bridging strong AI models with great software is how we solve real-world challenges.

I found[Dario Amodei](https://www.linkedin.com/in/dario-amodei-3934934?trk=public_post-text)’s blog post to have mixed undertones. While he commends the innovative engineering techniques employed by[DeepSeek AI](https://cn.linkedin.com/company/deepseek-ai?trk=public_post-text), he simultaneously downplays its performance by highlighting that benchmarks were conducted using DeepSeek’s latest release compared to 7–8-month-old U.S. models.
- Furthermore, the post’s attempt to justify the decreasing costs of building AI (a 4x reduction per year) and to validate DeepSeek’s development costs ($6–7M) as reasonable felt more like an effort to reassure investors than a neutral analysis.
- Similarly, the argument that the U.S. should retain AI leadership because AI is controlled by a non-democratic party in China did not come across as a compelling point.
That said, I’m excited about the prospect of a solution that’s 10x better than DeepSeek AI at 4x lower costs!

OpenAI is preparing to release its next-generation reasoning model, tentatively named "o3." This model aims to enhance AI's problem-solving capabilities by allowing it to spend more time deliberating on user queries before responding, thereby improving accuracy and reasoning depth. The decision to name the model "o3" stems from potential trademark conflicts with "o2," a name associated with a British telecommunications service provider. To avoid these issues, OpenAI is considering skipping directly to "o3." This development follows the recent release of OpenAI's o1 model, which marked a significant advancement in AI reasoning. The o1 model demonstrated improved performance in complex tasks, including mathematics, coding, and scientific problem-solving, by employing a "chain of thought" process that allows the AI to internally deliberate before providing answers. The upcoming o3 model is expected to build upon these capabilities, further enhancing AI's ability to handle complex reasoning tasks. This progression aligns with industry trends, as other tech giants like Google have also introduced their own reasoning AI models, such as Gemini 2.0 Flash Thinking, which employs similar techniques to achieve deeper problem-solving abilities. While specific details about the o3 model's features and release date are yet to be announced, OpenAI's continued focus on developing advanced reasoning models underscores its commitment to pushing the boundaries of AI capabilities.

This pattern has played out before. Farming. Printing. Electricity. The internet. Every time, the people who adapted early thrived. 𝗖𝗼𝘀𝘁 𝗵𝗮𝘀 𝗰𝗼𝗹𝗹𝗮𝗽𝘀𝗲𝗱 - AI that cost millions now costs pennies. Access is no longer a barrier. 2. 𝗤𝘂𝗮𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗰𝗿𝗼𝘀𝘀𝗲𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗯𝗮𝗿 - These systems aren't toys anymore. "The Urgency of Interpretability" by Dario Amodei

Dario Amodei, CEO of Anthropic, argues that understanding the inner workings of advanced AI systems-known as interpretability-is now an urgent priority as AI models rapidly become more powerful and autonomous. He likens the current state of AI to a "black box," where even creators cannot fully explain why models make certain decisions. This unprecedented opacity poses significant risks, including the inability to predict or prevent misaligned, deceptive, or dangerous behaviors in AI systems.
Podcast Version: The Urgency of Interpretability

- Opaque Decision-Making: Unlike traditional software, generative AI systems develop their own internal logic through training, making their reasoning processes unpredictable and hard to audit.
- Risk Management: Many concerns about AI safety-such as models taking unintended harmful actions, being jailbroken, or being misused-stem from this lack of transparency. Without interpretability, it's difficult to detect, diagnose, or mitigate these risks.
- Barriers to Adoption: The inability to explain AI decisions prevents their use in high-stakes domains (e.g., finance, law, medicine), where legal or ethical standards require transparency.
- Societal Implications: Opacity also complicates debates about AI sentience and rights, and undermines public trust and regulatory oversight.

- Early Achievements: Initial work in mechanistic interpretability identified neurons in vision models that mapped to human-understandable concepts, like "car detector" or "wheel detector." Later, similar approaches were applied to language models.
- Superposition Problem: Most neurons in large models represent a confusing mix of many concepts, a phenomenon called "superposition." This makes it extremely difficult to map model internals to human-understandable ideas.
- Recent Breakthroughs: Techniques like sparse autoencoders now allow researchers to identify combinations of neurons (called "features") that correspond to clearer concepts, and to manipulate these features to observe their effects. Automation and AI-assisted analysis (autointerpretability) have enabled the identification of millions of such features, though models likely contain billions.
- Tracing Reasoning: Researchers can now trace "circuits"-groups of features that represent reasoning steps in models-offering a glimpse into how AI arrives at answers. However, scaling this process to cover the full complexity of modern models remains a major challenge.

The Strawberry Problem: When AI's Confidence Doesn't… Michael Alf 11 months ago
No, It’s Not Really AI Yet Jack Skeels 4 months ago
Why Explainability Is the New Competitive Edge in 2025 Sharma Saravanan 7 months ago

- Diagnosing Model Flaws: Interpretability tools have been used in experiments to detect deliberately introduced alignment problems in AI models, showing promise for practical risk reduction.
- Aspirational Goal: Amodei envisions a future where AI models can be "scanned" like an MRI, reliably revealing issues such as deception, power-seeking, or cognitive weaknesses before deployment. This would be integral to AI safety protocols.
- Race Against Time: AI capabilities are advancing so rapidly that there is a real risk interpretability research will lag behind, leaving humanity unable to audit or control the most powerful systems. Amodei sets an ambitious goal for Anthropic: to reliably detect most model problems by 2027.

- Research Investment: Amodei urges AI companies, academia, and governments to prioritize and resource interpretability research, making it a foundational part of AI development and regulation.
- Collaboration: He advocates for industry-wide cooperation, standardized tools, and benchmarks to make interpretability scalable and robust, rather than a niche academic pursuit.
- Societal Stakes: The essay concludes with a warning: failing to achieve interpretability risks creating a world governed by systems we cannot understand or control. Success, on the other hand, would transform AI from a black box into a transparent, trustworthy partner in human progress.
> "I consider it basically unacceptable for humanity to be totally ignorant of how [advanced AI systems] work... We are thus in a race between interpretability and model intelligence." - Dario Amodei
In summary, Amodei’s essay is a clarion call for urgent, collective action to make AI systems interpretable-before their complexity outpaces our ability to understand or govern them.

Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei pens a smart look at our AI future

Welcome to AI Decoded, Fast Company’s weekly newsletter that breaks down the most important news in the world of AI. I’m Mark Sullivan, a senior writer at Fast Company, covering emerging tech, AI, and tech policy.
This week, I’m focusing on a powerful new essay by one of the brightest minds in AI, Anthropic’s Dario Amodei. I also look at the debate over whether AI really “reasons,” and at Perplexity’s problem with the New York Times.
Sign up to receive this newsletter every week via email here. And if you have comments on this issue and/or ideas for future ones, drop me a line at sullivan@fastcompany.com, and follow me on X (formerly Twitter)@thesullivan.
---

In the debate over artificial general intelligence, it’s often the “doomers” (Eliezer Yudkowsky) or showmen (OpenAI CEO Sam Altman, X CEO Elon Musk) making the most noise. But many of these viewpoints—whether optimistic or pessimistic—are ultimately vague and abstract. That’s why it’s worth listening to people like Dario Amodei.
Amodei and his company, Anthropic, have spent lots of time and money erecting safeguards against the potential harms of AI. In his new essay, “Machines of Loving Grace,” Amodei explores the most likely ways that superintelligence—that is, AI that exceeds human intelligence—might bring about measurable positive change. In the essay, he describes what superintelligence, or “strong AI” as he calls it, will look like, and how it might begin to enable progress in such fields as biology and neuroscience that will “directly improve the quality of human life.”
Strong AI could show up as early as 2026, Amodei believes. This model could look similar to today’s large language models, he posits, or it might consist of a system of interacting models that are trained differently than the LLMs we know today. The system will be smarter than the Nobel Prize winners in various fields, he says, and will access all “interfaces” available to a human working in a digital domain (text, audio, video, internet, etc.). Strong AI will be able to control robots and other equipment, he says, and work through large, complex problems autonomously. The model also will be able to share its training data with other models, creating potentially thousands of superintelligent AIs within a data center.
The essay gets even more interesting when Amodei’s focus leaves the data center. He notes that, in biology, life-saving advances are hindered by a lack of reliable data about complex systems. Amodei considers, as an example, the vast complexity of the human metabolism. “[I]t’s very hard to isolate the effect of any part of this complex system, and even harder to intervene on the system in a precise or predictable way,” he writes. Modeling such biological systems involves lots of “wet lab” work by humans, and it's a slow process. AI can do far more than just analyze or look for patterns in existing data, but rather can act as a “principal investigator” that plans, directs, and manages new research projects (perhaps conducted by robots).
Click here to read more about Dario Amodei’s essay on AGI.

One of the biggest debates in AI circles is whether or not current AI systems can truly “reason.” OpenAI claims that its most recent model, the o1 series, can “think” about various approaches to problem-solving and select the best one. But the o1 model is still a large language model; it uses complex math to work out the most probable next word in a sequence. How can that constitute reasoning?

What If AI Was a Great “What If” Moment? …Or What If… Laure Crane 11 months ago
A Spectrum of Artificial General Intelligence? Robert Hercock 1 year ago
The Dawn of a New Digital Species: Grappling with the… Phillip Alcock 1 year ago
Ilya Sutskever, the recently departed OpenAI cofounder, says that by predicting a lot of next words, the model can indeed “reason” through complex problems. In a recent fireside chat with Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang, Sutskever offered a thought exercise: “Say you read a detective novel, and on the last page, the detective says, ‘I am going to reveal the identity of the criminal, and that person's name is _____.’. . . predict that word.” This suggests that by processing the meaning and order of the words in the novel, the LLM learns enough to generate deep insights.
One of the pioneers of modern neural networks, Meta’s chief AI scientist Yann LeCun, told me back in 2019 that large language models (we were talking about “RoBERTa” at the time) can indeed learn about the world from processing huge amounts of training data on hundreds of servers for months at a time. An AI model might understand, for example, that when a car drives off a cliff it doesn’t just hang in the air, but plummets to the rocks below.
Click here to read more about Yann LeCun’s skepticism of AI’s ability to reason.

First, the New York Times sued OpenAI for using its content for LLM training purposes. Now the Grey Lady has sent a cease-and-desist letter to the AI search startup Perplexity, demanding that it stop using Times content in its custom answers to user queries.
The Times also gave Perplexity until the end of the month to explain how it is still accessing Times content after promising earlier that its web crawlers would stop doing so. Perplexity’s “answer engine” works by crawling large swaths of information on the web and then creating a big database (an index) of content it grabs from web pages. Pieces of that content are then used to form custom answers to user queries.
Click here to read more about the New York Times’ cease-and-desist letter to Perplexity.
